sentence_length.why: Matches read-aloud breath and 3-6 year attention span
sentence_length.example_good: She pulled her yellow coat tight and took one step.
sentence_length.example_bad: She grabbed her yellow raincoat and pulled it tight around her shoulders before taking the first big step through the door.

vocabulary.rule: Substitute any word a 4-year-old would not know
vocabulary.test: If you wouldn't hear it on a playground, cut it
vocabulary.allowed_exceptions: One new word per book maximum — define it through context, not explanation
vocabulary.example_good: Her heart went fast. Very, very fast.
vocabulary.example_bad: Her pulse accelerated with anticipatory anxiety.

rhythm.rule: Read every 3 sentences aloud. Does it breathe?
rhythm.target_pace: Full picture book: 3–5 minutes at a natural parent bedtime pace
rhythm.cadence: Vary sentence length. Short. Then medium length. Then short again.
rhythm.read_aloud_test: If you stumble while reading, rewrite that sentence — stumbling means the rhythm is broken

emotion_naming.rule: Name the feeling explicitly at least once per book
emotion_naming.why: Emotional vocabulary is the #1 developmental goal — parents buy this book for this reason
emotion_naming.example_good: That feeling in her tummy? That was worry. And worry was okay.
emotion_naming.note: Name it plainly. Do not euphemize or over-poeticize the core emotion.

repetition_device.rule: Use a refrain — a phrase that repeats 2–4 times through the book
repetition_device.why: Children expect and love repetition. It creates rhythm and memorability. Parents remember it too.
repetition_device.placement: Introduce refrain on spread 2–3. Repeat at the low point. Deliver it with new meaning on the final spread.

ending_tone.rule: Final spread must be: warm + concrete + hopeful
ending_tone.warm: The child feels seen, not lectured
ending_tone.concrete: A specific action or image — not abstract reassurance
ending_tone.hopeful: The world is good. The child has what they need.
ending_tone.example_good: She still felt the butterflies. But now she knew — butterflies meant something exciting was starting.
ending_tone.example_bad: And she learned that it was good to be brave and share her feelings with others.

parent_layer.rule: There must be a second meaning that makes a parent catch their breath
parent_layer.why: Parents are reading this too. Give them something. This is what makes a book go viral.
parent_layer.test: Read the last spread. Does it hit differently for a grown-up?
parent_layer.example: A book about leaving home for school is also about every parent's fear of letting go

voice_checklist: [ ] Zero words a 4-year-old wouldn't know (or 1 new word taught through context)
[ ] Timed read: 3–5 minutes (picture book) / under 1 minute (board book)
[ ] Feeling named explicitly at least once
[ ] Refrain appears 2–4 times and lands differently on final use
[ ] Final spread is warm + concrete + hopeful
[ ] Parent layer present — at least one line that hits an adult differently

pillar_voice_notes.P1_emotional_intelligence: Name the feeling early. Don't resolve it too fast — let the child sit in it for 2–3 spreads. The resolution comes from within the character, not from an adult fixing it.
pillar_voice_notes.P2_life_skills: Make the task feel like an adventure, not a chore. The child protagonist should want to do the thing by the end — not just comply.
pillar_voice_notes.P3_wonder_stem: Lead with the question, not the answer. Let the wonder breathe for 2 spreads before the explanation. End with a new question — curiosity compounds.
pillar_voice_notes.P4_belonging: Validate the hard feeling first. Do NOT rush to reassurance. The belonging must be earned through the story, not stated by an adult character.

age_band_voice_notes.board_book: One noun or one emotion per page. No sentences — labels only, or 3-word max phrases. Every page is a standalone unit.
age_band_voice_notes.picture_book: Full arc in 12–16 spreads. One sentence per page, two max. The illustrations carry 70% of the story — write for what you see, not what you explain.
age_band_voice_notes.early_reader: Short chapters of 2–4 pages each. First-person or close third. Internal thoughts visible. Dialogue drives pace. End each chapter with a micro-cliffhanger or question.
